entry.title: Inside SAP Build at SAP Sapphire 2026: What to Expect from AI Agents and Agentic Development
entry.content:
SAP Sapphire is almost here, and SAP Build at Sapphire is where customers and partners will be able to see how to easily create and deploy AI agents starting with simple language descriptions.  

For anyone tracking where AI agents and agentic development are headed in the enterprise, SAP Build will offer a chance to see it up close. Attendees will be able to experience how SAP Build helps teams build AI agents across SAP. This includes hands-on experience, live demos, sessions, and direct access to the teams building these capabilities. 

This year, the focus is on making agentic development real - moving from intent to working solution through a guided, end-to-end experience. 

 The hands-on experience: Build an agent with Joule Studio 

The biggest draw for SAP Build at Sapphire will be the hands-on experience on the show floor. 

Attendees can sit down at an open station and work through guided scenarios in a live system – building agents with Joule Studio, generating app logic from intent, and integrating with SAP data and workflows. This is a practical view at how agentic development works from start to deployment. 

This experience builds on the popular SAP Build Developer Garage at TechEd, which became a go-to stop for developers who wanted to get hands-on and see SAP Build in action. At Sapphire, we are bringing that energy back and making it bigger for a broader audience. 

Space will be limited, and access will be first-come, first-serve, so this is one stop worth making early. 

The sessions 

SAP Build sessions at Sapphire will focus on how teams are building AI agents, clean-core extensions and automation workflows. You’ll see how agentic development is evolving - from defining intent to generating and deploying working solutions - and how Joule is integrated across the development lifecycle. 

For anyone trying to separate AI hype from AI execution, these sessions are worth prioritizing. 

The booth 

The SAP Build and Agentic AI booths are where live walk-throughs and real conversations happen. 

Bring your own use cases, connect directly with SAP experts, and validate how SAP Build fits into your architecture. Whether you are building your first extension or scaling AI-driven workflows, the booth’s are designed for both strategic and practical discussions.  

Expect demos, conversations, and a chance to get closer to what SAP Build is showing at Sapphire. 

 

Why stop by SAP Build at SAP Sapphire 

AI agents are a major focus at Sapphire but SAP Build is where you can see how they are actually built and deployed in SAP environments. 

Get hands-on. Talk to the experts. See how agentic development works in practice. 

Plan your SAP Build stop at SAP Sapphire: 

  • Register for SAP Sapphire 
  • Browse SAP Build sessions 
  • Find the SAP Build booth (see the map onsite)

entry.title: Experiment with SAP RPT-1: Predicting the Right Carrier in SAP TM Using the RPT-1 API Token
entry.content:
In my previous blog about experimenting with RPT-1, we explored how to try out using the RPT-1 playground version. Now, let’s see how this can be done programmatically. Please check the  SAP Cloud SDK for Python for RPT-1 native APIs. We will first use request library to call the API directly.

Prerequisites:

  1. An API key for the service – you can get it from the ‘Your API Token’ section at https://rpt.cloud.sap/docs.

 

 

 

We will use the same .csv file used previously for the Freight Orders with ‘[PREDICT]’ tag.

RPT-1 API accept json format. So, lets first convert .csv file to json format.

#Read csv and convert to json
import csv
import json
def csv_to_json(csv_file_path):

    data = []
    with open(csv_file_path, encoding='utf-8') as csv_file:
       reader = csv.DictReader(csv_file) 
        for row in reader:
            data.append(row
    final_payload = {
        "prediction_config": {
            "target_columns": [
                {
                    "name": "Carrier",
                    "prediction_placeholder": "[PREDICT]"
                }
            ]
        },
        "index_column": "Document" ,
        "rows": data         
    }
    print(f"Done! {len(data)} records converted to json")
    return final_payload

 

Prepare the request parameters

access_token = "<<Your access token here>>" #Use .env for secure storage
url = "https://rpt.cloud.sap/api/predict"

Now call the API

import requests
import json
headers = {
    "Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}",
    "Content-Type": "application/json",
    "Accept": "application/json",
}
final_payload = csv_to_json('sample.csv')
response = requests.post(url, headers=headers, json=final_payload, timeout=120)
response.raise_for_status()
data_pred = response.json()
preds = data_pred.get("prediction", [])
print(json.dumps(preds, indent=2, ensure_ascii=False))

The result would look like this.

{
  "id": "ba15b9bd-8cde-4eee-b858-0329f1b48e11",
  "metadata": {
    "num_columns": 38,
    "num_predictions": 1,
    "num_query_rows": 1,
    "num_rows": 334
  },
  "predictions": [
    {
      "Carrier": [
        {
          "confidence": 0.46,
          "confidence_interval": null,
          "prediction": "MM_CAR_01"
        }
      ],
      "Document": 6100000600
    }
  ],
  "status": {
    "code": 0,
    "message": "ok"
  }
}

If you have RPT-1 deployment in SAP AI Launchpad, then you can use native API call.

import os
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.native.sap.models import RPTRequest, PredictionConfig, TargetColumn
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.native.sap.client import RPTClient
#Get the credentials from the AI Core instance in your BTP sub-account
os.environ["AICORE_AUTH_URL"] = "<<AICORE_AUTH_URL>>oauth/token"
os.environ["AICORE_CLIENT_ID"] = "<<AICORE_CLIENT_ID>>"
os.environ["AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET"] = "<<AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET>>"
os.environ["AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP"] = "<<AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP>>"
os.environ["AICORE_BASE_URL"] = "<<AICORE_BASE_URL>>/v2"

final_payload = csv_to_json('sample.csv', target_columns=["Carrier"])

client = RPTClient()
body = RPTRequest(
            prediction_config=PredictionConfig(
                target_columns=[
                    TargetColumn(name="Carrier", task_type="classification")
                ]),
            rows=final_payload
        )
response = client.predict(body=body, model_name="sap-rpt-1-small")
print(response.predictions)

This example shows using Python, but this can be done in ABAP as well using HTTP request call (such as a save process controller strategy method in SAP TM). 

What you learned

  • How to shape tabular data for SAP RPT‑1 with a clear prediction_config and index_column
  • How to invoke the predict API(using request call and native API) securely and read confidence‑scored outputs
  • We used 'predicting carrier' just as an example and to understand the usage. You can think of any other use cases which require prediction from minimal data set which doesn't require complex classic ML training.

entry.title: SAP RPT-1 Meets Employee Attrition: A Hands-On Test with SuccessFactors Data
entry.content:
The Short Version

I wanted to see if SAP’s new tabular AI model, RPT-1, could predict which employees are likely to quit, using nothing but a CSV extracted from SuccessFactors fields. No model training. No data science team. Just upload and predict.

To keep things honest, I based the experiment on a real peer-reviewed study that used actual employee data and traditional ML. The result? RPT-1 nailed all 5 test predictions in under 400 milliseconds. But the full story is more interesting than the headline.

 

Why I Picked Attrition (and Not Another Sales Demo)

Credit where it's due: Hari Panakkal's recent SAP Community post, Experiment with SAP RPT-1: Predicting the Right Carrier in SAP TM Using the No-Code Playground, is a great walkthrough of RPT-1 on transportation data. It got me thinking: if RPT-1 works that well for carrier prediction in TM, what happens when you point it at an HR problem?

Most RPT-1 demos I've seen focus on sales forecasting or logistics. Those are solid use cases, but the one I care about is people analytics, and the single biggest question HR teams ask is: "Who's about to leave?"

Attrition is a natural fit for RPT-1 because:

  • The data is tabular and already sitting in SuccessFactors
  • The problem is binary classification (stays vs. leaves)
  • Most HR teams don't have ML engineers on staff

But I didn't want to just throw random HR data at it. I wanted to test RPT-1 against something with a known answer.

 

The Research Behind the Experiment

I grounded everything in this study:

Manafi Varkiani, S., Pattarin, F., Fabbri, T., & Fantoni, G. (2025). Predicting employee attrition and explaining its determinants. Expert Systems With Applications, 272, 126575.

Here’s what makes this paper worth paying attention to:

It used real data. Not overused Kaggle dataset, but actual records from an Italian financial company. 5,767 employees. 309 voluntary resignations (a 5.4% attrition rate, which is realistic and makes the problem genuinely hard).

They tested four algorithms head-to-head. Random Forest crushed everything: 99.9% AUC, 94.4% sensitivity. Logistic Regression and Decision Trees weren’t even close.

They found things that don’t match your intuition. This is the best part. Using SHAP analysis, they discovered that employees who received bonuses were more likely to leave (burnout from overperforming), and employees who got retention incentives also showed higher attrition. It turns out retention incentives are targeted at people already flagged as flight risks, so the incentive is a signal of existing risk, not a cause of it.

The ML pipeline was serious work. ROSE resampling for class imbalance, 10-fold cross-validation, hyperparameter tuning, and SHAP post-processing. This took significant data science effort.

So the question became: Can RPT-1 get comparable results with a single API call?

 

Mapping the Variables to SuccessFactors

The study used 14 employee attributes. I mapped each one to the actual OData entity and field in SuccessFactors, and validated every mapping against the live API:

 

Research Variable

Where It Lives in SF

Category

Gender

PerPersonal.gender

Personal

Age Class

Derived from PerPerson.dateOfBirth

Personal

Tenure Class

Derived from EmpEmployment.startDate

People

Department

EmpJob.department

Work Environment

Rank

EmpJob.payGrade → FOPayGrade (grouped)

Management

Contract Type

EmpJob.contractType

Management

Job Level

EmpJob.payGrade

Management

Talent Program

TalentPool membership

People

Bonus Received

EmpPayCompNonRecurring existence

Management

Hours (PT/FT)

EmpJob.standardHours

Management

Retention Incentive

Custom MDF or EmpPayCompRecurring

Management

Job Family

FOJobCode.jobFunction

Management

Log Salary

EmpPayCompRecurring.paycompvalue (log10)

Management

Log Premium

Performance bonus (log10)

Management

Picture 1: Ishikawa – Fishbone diagram

 

What Is RPT-1, Exactly?

SAP RPT-1 is a Relational Pretrained Transformer , a foundation model built for tabular data, not text. Announced at TechEd 2025, it comes in three flavors:

  • sap-rpt-1-small : Fast commercial model (low latency)
  • sap-rpt-1-large : Higher accuracy commercial model
  • sap-rpt-1-oss : Open-source on GitHub (Apache 2.0)

The key idea: you give it labeled rows as examples and rows with [PREDICT] placeholders as questions. It learns from the context: no training, no feature engineering, no resampling. Think of it like few-shot prompting in ChatGPT, but for spreadsheets.

 

Running the Experiment

Step 1: Build the Dataset

I created sf_employee_attrition.csv with 50 labeled employee records using the 14 features from the research, plus 5 query rows with [PREDICT] in the ATTRITION column. The labeled data reflects the patterns the study found: higher attrition among younger, shorter-tenure employees with lower compensation, and the counterintuitive bonus/retention effects.

 

Picture 2 - sf_employee_attrition.csv, 50 labeled + 5 query rows, 14 features mapped to SF fields

 

Step 2: The No-Code Route (Playground)

Easiest way to test. Go to rpt.cloud.sap/dashboard:

  1. Upload the CSV
  2. The interface detects the [PREDICT] cells
  3. Click Predict
  4. Done

No coding. Supports up to 2,073 rows and 50 columns.

 

Step 3: The API Route (for Pipelines)

This option allows full control and evaluation metrics:

"""
SAP RPT-1 Employee Attrition Prediction
========================================
This script demonstrates how to use SAP-RPT-1 (Relational Pretrained Transformer)
to predict employee attrition using structured HR data modeled after
SuccessFactors workforce analytics dimensions.

Dataset design informed by: Manafi Varkiani et al. (2025),
"Predicting employee attrition and explaining its determinants",
Expert Systems With Applications, 272, 126575.


Prerequisites:
  - For API approach: SAP RPT Playground account (https://rpt.cloud.sap)
  - For OSS approach: Python 3.11+, GPU with 24-80GB VRAM recommended

Author: Shakti Barath
"""

import json
import csv
import requests
import os
from pathlib import Path

# ============================================================
# CONFIGURATION
# ============================================================

# Option A: Load token from file (recommended)
# Create access_token.json with: {"token": "YOUR_TOKEN_HERE"}
# Get your token from: https://rpt.cloud.sap/docs (scroll to bottom)
TOKEN_FILE = "access_token.json"

# Option B: Set as environment variable
# export SAP_RPT_TOKEN="your_token_here"

RPT_API_URL = "https://rpt.cloud.sap/api/predict"
CSV_FILE = "sf_employee_attrition.csv"
TARGET_COLUMN = "ATTRITION"
PREDICT_PLACEHOLDER = "[PREDICT]"

def load_token():
    """Load API token from file or environment variable."""
    if os.path.exists(TOKEN_FILE):
        with open(TOKEN_FILE) as f:
            data = json.load(f)
            return data.get("token", "")
    return os.environ.get("SAP_RPT_TOKEN", "")

def load_csv_as_rows(filepath):
    """
    Load CSV and convert to RPT-1 row format.
    
    RPT-1 expects rows as a list of dicts. Rows with [PREDICT] in the 
    target column serve as query rows; all others are context rows
    (few-shot examples for in-context learning).
    """
    rows = []
    with open(filepath, newline="", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        reader = csv.DictReader(f)
        for row in reader:
            rows.append(dict(row))
    return rows

def build_payload(rows, target_column, index_column="EMP_ID"):
    """
    Build the RPT-1 API request payload.
    
    Structure:
    - rows: list of dicts (context + query rows)
    - index_column: unique identifier column
    - prediction_config: specifies target and task type
    
    Query rows contain [PREDICT] in the target column.
    Context rows contain actual labels — these are the few-shot examples.
    """
    payload = {
        "rows": rows,
        "index_column": index_column,
        "prediction_config": {
            "target_columns": [
                {
                    "name": target_column,
                    "placeholder_value": PREDICT_PLACEHOLDER,
                    "task_type": "classification"
                }
            ]
        }
    }
    return payload

def call_rpt1_api(payload, token):
    """Send prediction request to SAP RPT-1 Playground API."""
    headers = {
        "Authorization": f"Bearer {token}",
        "Content-Type": "application/json",
        "Accept": "application/json"
    }
    
    print("Sending prediction request to SAP RPT-1...")
    print(f"  Context rows: {sum(1 for r in payload['rows'] if r.get(TARGET_COLUMN) != PREDICT_PLACEHOLDER)}")
    print(f"  Query rows:   {sum(1 for r in payload['rows'] if r.get(TARGET_COLUMN) == PREDICT_PLACEHOLDER)}")
    
    response = requests.post(RPT_API_URL, headers=headers, json=payload, timeout=120)
    response.raise_for_status()
    return response.json()

def display_predictions(result, rows):
    """Display RPT-1 predictions alongside employee context."""
    predictions = result.get("prediction", {}).get("predictions", [])
    
    # Build lookup for employee context
    emp_lookup = {str(r["EMP_ID"]): r for r in rows}
    
    print("\n" + "=" * 80)
    print("SAP RPT-1 ATTRITION PREDICTIONS")
    print("=" * 80)
    
    for pred in predictions:
        emp_id = str(pred.get("id", ""))
        emp = emp_lookup.get(emp_id, {})
        attrition_pred = pred.get(TARGET_COLUMN, [{}])
        
        if isinstance(attrition_pred, list) and len(attrition_pred) > 0:
            predicted_value = attrition_pred[0].get("prediction", "N/A")
            confidence = attrition_pred[0].get("confidence")
        else:
            predicted_value = "N/A"
            confidence = None
        
        risk_indicator = "HIGH RISK" if predicted_value == "Yes" else "LOW RISK"
        
        print(f"\n  Employee {emp_id}: {emp.get('JOB_FAMILY', 'Unknown')}")
        print(f"  Department: {emp.get('DEPARTMENT', '-')} | "
              f"Age: {emp.get('AGE_CLASS', '-')} | "
              f"Tenure: {emp.get('TENURE_CLASS', '-')}")
        print(f"  Talent Program: {emp.get('TALENT_PROGRAM', '-')} | "
              f"Contract: {emp.get('CONTRACT_TYPE', '-')}")
        print(f"  Prediction: {predicted_value} ({risk_indicator})")
        if confidence is not None:
            print(f"  Confidence: {confidence:.2%}")
    
    print("\n" + "=" * 80)

def analyze_context_patterns(rows):
    """
    Analyze attrition patterns in context data.
    Mirrors the descriptive statistics approach from the research paper
    (Manafi Varkiani et al., 2025, Table 6).
    """
    context_rows = [r for r in rows if r.get(TARGET_COLUMN) != PREDICT_PLACEHOLDER]
    
    print("\n" + "-" * 60)
    print("CONTEXT DATA ANALYSIS (mirrors research methodology)")
    print("-" * 60)
    
    # Attrition by age class
    age_stats = {}
    for r in context_rows:
        age = r["AGE_CLASS"]
        left = r[TARGET_COLUMN] == "Yes"
        if age not in age_stats:
            age_stats[age] = {"total": 0, "left": 0}
        age_stats[age]["total"] += 1
        age_stats[age]["left"] += int(left)
    
    print("\nAttrition Rate by Age Class:")
    for age in sorted(age_stats.keys()):
        s = age_stats[age]
        rate = (s["left"] / s["total"] * 100) if s["total"] > 0 else 0
        print(f"  {age:12s}: {rate:5.1f}%  ({s['left']}/{s['total']})")
    
    # Attrition by tenure class
    tenure_stats = {}
    for r in context_rows:
        tenure = r["TENURE_CLASS"]
        left = r[TARGET_COLUMN] == "Yes"
        if tenure not in tenure_stats:
            tenure_stats[tenure] = {"total": 0, "left": 0}
        tenure_stats[tenure]["total"] += 1
        tenure_stats[tenure]["left"] += int(left)
    
    print("\nAttrition Rate by Tenure Class:")
    for tenure in sorted(tenure_stats.keys()):
        s = tenure_stats[tenure]
        rate = (s["left"] / s["total"] * 100) if s["total"] > 0 else 0
        print(f"  {tenure:12s}: {rate:5.1f}%  ({s['left']}/{s['total']})")
    
    # Key insight from the research
    print("\n  Key Insight (Manafi Varkiani et al., 2025):")
    print("  Younger employees and those with shorter tenure show")
    print("  significantly higher attrition rates. The research found")
    print("  15.9% attrition for age 18-25 vs 1.4% for age 50-65.")

# ============================================================
# MAIN EXECUTION
# ============================================================

def main():
    print("=" * 80)
    print("SAP RPT-1: Employee Attrition Prediction Experiment")
    print("Grounded in: Manafi Varkiani et al. (2025), ESWA 272")
    print("=" * 80)
    
    # Load data
    if not Path(CSV_FILE).exists():
        print(f"ERROR: {CSV_FILE} not found. Place it in the same directory.")
        return
    
    rows = load_csv_as_rows(CSV_FILE)
    print(f"\nLoaded {len(rows)} employee records from {CSV_FILE}")
    
    # Analyze context patterns
    analyze_context_patterns(rows)
    
    # Load token
    token = load_token()
    if not token:
        print("\n" + "-" * 60)
        print("NO API TOKEN FOUND")
        print("-" * 60)
        print("To run predictions with the RPT Playground API:")
        print("1. Sign up at https://rpt.cloud.sap")
        print("2. Copy your token from https://rpt.cloud.sap/docs")
        print("3. Save it to access_token.json:")
        print('   {"token": "YOUR_TOKEN_HERE"}')
        print("\nAlternatively, use the OSS version (see rpt1_oss_predict.py)")
        return
    
    # Build payload and predict
    payload = build_payload(rows, TARGET_COLUMN)
    
    try:
        result = call_rpt1_api(payload, token)
        display_predictions(result, rows)
        
        # Save raw response
        with open("rpt1_predictions.json", "w") as f:
            json.dump(result, f, indent=2)
        print(f"\nRaw predictions saved to rpt1_predictions.json")
        
    except requests.exceptions.HTTPError as e:
        print(f"\nAPI Error: {e}")
        print("Check your token and ensure the playground is accessible.")
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"\nError: {e}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

Create access_token.json in the same folder with your RPT-1 API key:

{
  "token": "YOUR_RPT-API_KEY"
}

 

The Results

I ran the CSV through the RPT Playground API. Here’s exactly what came back:

EMP ID

Profile

Prediction

Confidence

Why It Makes Sense

1051

Finance • Staff • Permanent

Yes

95%

Age 25–30, Tenure 0–3 yr, Talent Program, Low premium

1052

IT • Staff • Permanent

No

98%

Age 30–40, Tenure 10–20 yr, Bonus received, Strong salary

1053

Sales • Staff • Fixed-Term

Yes

99%

Age 18–25, Tenure 0–3 yr, Fixed-term, Lowest salary

1054

HR • Manager • Permanent

No

99%

Age 40–50, Tenure 10–20 yr, Manager rank, High comp

1055

Ops • Staff • Permanent

Yes

94%

Age 25–30, Talent Prog, Retention incentive, Lower salary

 

Accuracy

Avg Confidence

Inference Time

Context Rows

5/5 (100%)

97%

399 ms

50 labeled rows

 

What Stood Out

The easy calls were easy. Employee 1053 (young, short-tenure, fixed-term, lowest salary) and Employee 1054 (senior manager, long tenure, high pay) both got 99% confidence. These are the extremes, exactly where the research paper’s Random Forest also showed the clearest separation.

Employee 1055 was the interesting one. This person has moderate tenure (3–10 years), which in the training data strongly correlates with staying. But RPT-1 correctly flagged them as at-risk at 94% confidence. Why? The combination of a retention incentive, talent program membership, and lower compensation. Remember what the research found: retention incentives are a signal that someone is already a flight risk, not a shield against it. RPT-1 picked up that interaction from just 50 context rows. That’s impressive.

399 milliseconds. The research paper required ROSE resampling, 10-fold cross-validation, hyperparameter tuning across four algorithms, and SHAP post-processing. RPT-1 got directionally equivalent predictions with a single API call on raw CSV data. For an HR team that wants a quick risk screening before committing to a full ML build, that’s a big deal.

The honest caveat: This is a 50-row synthetic dataset with intentionally clear patterns. Production data with 5,767 employees and a 5.4% attrition rate would be a harder test. These results show RPT-1 can read tabular patterns well. A head-to-head on real production data is the next step.

 

What RPT-1 Is Good At Here

  • No ML pipeline required. The research team spent serious effort on resampling, cross-validation, and algorithm selection. RPT-1 skips all of that.
  • Handles mixed data out of the box. Categorical and numerical fields go in as-is. No encoding needed.
  • Tolerates missing data. The research had to drop 5 variables due to gaps. RPT-1 handles missing values by design.
  • Easy to iterate. Want to add engagement survey scores? Just add a column. No pipeline rebuild.

 

What RPT-1 Can’t Do (Yet)

  • No explainability. RPT-1 gives you a prediction and a confidence score, but not why. For HR decisions, that matters a lot.
  • Class imbalance is your problem. With a 5.4% attrition rate, your context rows need careful curation.
  • No time-series awareness. RPT-1 works on static snapshots. It can’t predict when someone will leave.
  • Playground has limits. 2,073 rows max. For production scale, deploy via SAP AI Core.

 

So What Should You Actually Do With This?

RPT-1 makes it trivially easy to generate attrition predictions from SuccessFactors data. But predictions without understanding can lead to bad interventions. (Remember the retention incentive example: a naïve reading suggests incentives cause attrition.)

My recommendation:

  1. Use RPT-1 as a rapid screening tool. Upload your SF extract, get a risk score, identify who needs a closer look.
  2. Build a traditional ML model in parallel for explainability. Run the same features through Random Forest with SHAP to understand the why.
  3. Loop in HR. Especially for counterintuitive patterns. Data literacy plus domain knowledge turns a prediction into an action.
  4. Don’t treat aggregate results as universal. Attrition drivers for one job family can differ completely from the company-wide pattern.

 

References

  • Manafi Varkiani, S., Pattarin, F., Fabbri, T., & Fantoni, G. (2025). Predicting employee attrition and explaining its determinants. Expert Systems With Applications, 272, 126575.
  • Lundberg, S. M., & Lee, S. I. (2017). A Unified Approach to Interpreting Model Predictions. NeurIPS 2017.
  • SAP-RPT-1: sap.com/products/artificial-intelligence/sap-rpt.html
  • SAP-RPT-1-OSS: github.com/SAP-samples/sap-rpt-1-oss
  • RPT Playground: rpt.cloud.sap
  • Reference Blog — Carrier Prediction in SAP TM: SAP Community (Mar 2026)

Happy experimenting!

Built with synthetic data mirroring the variable structure and patterns from Manafi Varkiani et al. (2025). No real employee data was used. Results are illustrative.

entry.title: SAP Agentic AI in Practice: Concepts, Architecture, and Your First LangGraph Agent (Part 1 of 6)
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY  |  SERIES 2: SAP AGENTIC AI IN PRACTICE  |  PART 1 OF 6
SAP Agentic AI in Practice: Concepts, Architecture, and Your First LangGraph Agent

What agentic AI actually means in SAP contexts, when to use an agent vs a skill vs a pipeline, the full agentic SAP stack with LangGraph and AI Core, and a working ReAct agent for SAP data — all in Part 1.

By Deepa Dorairaj   |  SAP AI Solution Architect  |  Series 2, Part 1 of 6  |  Published 2026

TL;DR: Agentic AI is not just a more capable pipeline. It's a fundamentally different execution model — the agent decides what to do next based on what it observes, rather than following a fixed sequence. This article covers what that means for SAP workloads specifically, when agents are the right choice vs skills and pipelines, the complete LangGraph + AI Core architecture for production SAP agents, and a working ReAct agent that queries SAP data, analyzes it, and produces findings autonomously.

Series 2 Context — Building on the Generative AI Hub Foundation

This is Part 1 of SAP Agentic AI in Practice — the second series building on the Generative AI Hub foundation. Series 1 covered setup, Python SDK, prompt pipelines, RAG on HANA Cloud, CAP applications, and Joule Studio. If you haven't read that series, the architecture knowledge from Parts 3–5 will be particularly useful background for this one.

Series 2 moves from pipelines to agents. The distinction matters — and Part 1 establishes it clearly before writing a single line of agent code. The four SAP use cases this series builds toward are: data migration landscape assessment, exception log triage, master data quality, and S/4HANA readiness. Each becomes a specialized agent. By Part 6 they work together as a coordinated multi-agent system.

PART A: CONCEPTS — WHAT AGENTIC AI ACTUALLY MEANS 

Agent vs Skill vs Pipeline — The Distinction That Matters

These three terms are used interchangeably in most SAP AI conversations. They shouldn't be. They represent fundamentally different execution models with different appropriate use cases.

Type

How It Works

SAP Example

Prompt Pipeline

Fixed sequence of prompts — output of one feeds the next. You define every step upfront. The model executes but doesn't decide.

Exception log triage with defined classify → summarize → recommend steps. Predictable, auditable, fast.

Joule Skill

Structured, deterministic capability triggered by user intent. Calls a specific API action. No reasoning or planning involved.

Answering a knowledge base question, looking up a material, creating a badge. Single-purpose, reliable.

AI Agent

Autonomous reasoner. Given a goal, the agent decides which tools to use, in what order, based on what it observes at each step. The sequence is not predetermined.

Assess an SAP landscape for S/4HANA readiness — the agent decides which systems to query, what to look for, and how to structure findings based on what it discovers.

The key differentiator is decision-making under uncertainty. Pipelines and skills are deterministic — given the same input, they always follow the same path. Agents are adaptive — they observe, reason, and decide what to do next based on what they find.

When to Use Each

Use a pipeline when the steps are known upfront and the output structure is fixed. Use a skill when the task is focused and the API call is well-defined. Use an agent when the task requires exploration, multi-step reasoning, or when the path to completion depends on what the agent discovers along the way.

Scenario

Use

SAP Example

Known steps, fixed output structure

Prompt pipeline

Exception log triage, document extraction

Single focused task, defined API

Joule skill

Material lookup, KB question, badge creation

Goal-directed, path unknown upfront

Agent

Landscape assessment, readiness analysis

Multi-step with dependencies on findings

Agent

Data quality sweep, migration risk scoring

Needs human approval at checkpoints

Agent with human-in-the-loop

Transport risk assessment, SoD conflict review

Agents are not always better than pipelines. They are more expensive (more LLM calls), less predictable (the path varies), and harder to debug. Use agents specifically when the task requires adaptive reasoning. For everything else, pipelines and skills are faster, cheaper, and more reliable.

PART B: ARCHITECTURE — THE AGENTIC SAP STACK 

The Complete LangGraph + AI Core Architecture for SAP Agents

Production SAP agents require four layers working together. LangGraph provides the agent orchestration framework. SAP AI Core provides governed foundation model access. HANA Cloud provides the data and vector layer. BTP Cloud Foundry provides the deployment and runtime environment.

LangGraph Agent → AI Core (foundation model) → SAP Tools (HANA, APIs) → HANA vector store → Findings output
Why LangGraph for SAP Agents

LangGraph is a graph-based agent orchestration framework built on LangChain. It models agent execution as a directed graph — nodes are actions or decisions, edges are transitions. This architecture makes it uniquely suited for SAP workloads for three reasons:

  • State persistence — LangGraph maintains agent state explicitly across steps. For SAP assessments that query multiple systems and accumulate findings, state persistence is essential.
  • Conditional routing — edges in the graph can be conditional. An agent can take different paths based on what it finds — escalate a critical finding, skip a check if a prerequisite fails, loop back for more data if confidence is low.
  • Human-in-the-loop support — LangGraph has native support for pausing execution and waiting for human approval before proceeding. For SAP processes where autonomous action carries risk, this is not optional.
The ReAct Pattern

ReAct (Reason + Act) is the foundational agent pattern used throughout this series. The agent alternates between two steps:

  • Reason — the model thinks about what it knows and what it needs to do next
  • Act — the model calls a tool (a function, an API, a database query) and observes the result

This cycle continues until the agent has enough information to produce a final answer or reaches a defined stopping condition. For SAP workloads, the tools are SAP-specific: HANA queries, RFC calls, AI Core model invocations, vector store retrievals.

Goal input → Reason: what do I know? → Act: call SAP tool → Observe result → Reason: what next? → Final findings
Agent State Schema for SAP Assessments

Every LangGraph agent requires an explicit state schema. For SAP assessment agents, the state carries the accumulated findings, queries run, systems checked, and confidence scores across all steps:

# Agent state schema — carries context across all reasoning steps

from typing import TypedDict, List, Optional

from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END

class SAPAssessmentState(TypedDict):

    goal:              str          # What the agent is trying to assess

    system_info:       dict         # SAP system metadata collected so far

    findings:          List[dict]   # Accumulated findings with severity

    queries_run:       List[str]    # Queries executed — for audit trail

    tools_used:        List[str]    # Tools called — for transparency

    confidence_score:  float        # Overall confidence in findings

    next_action:       str          # What the agent decided to do next

    human_review_needed: bool       # Flag for human-in-the-loop checkpoint

    final_report:      Optional[str] # Final output when complete
PART C: YOUR FIRST SAP AGENT — REACT WITH LANGGRAPH 

Building a ReAct Agent for SAP Data Assessment

This first agent demonstrates the complete ReAct pattern on a focused SAP task: given a HANA table name, autonomously assess its data quality — checking volume, null rates, duplicate rates, and anomalies — and produce a structured findings report. This is the foundation pattern that the more complex agents in Parts 2–6 build on.

Step 1 — Install Dependencies
# Install LangGraph and SAP AI SDK
pip install langgraph langchain-core generative-ai-hub-sdk hdbcli
Step 2 — Define SAP Tools

Tools are the functions the agent can call. Each tool has a name, description (used by the model to decide when to call it), and implementation. For SAP agents, tools wrap HANA queries and AI Core calls:

# tools.py — SAP tools available to the agent
from langchain_core.tools import tool
import hdbcli.dbapi as hdb
import json
 
# HANA connection — credentials from environment or .aicore-config.json
def get_hana_connection():
    return hdb.connect(
        address=os.environ['HANA_HOST'],
        port=443,
        user=os.environ['HANA_USER'],
        password=os.environ['HANA_PASSWORD'],
        encrypt=True
    )
 
@tool
def get_table_volume(table_name: str) -> str:
    """Get the row count and basic metadata for an SAP HANA table."""
    conn = get_hana_connection()
    cursor = conn.cursor()
    cursor.execute(f'SELECT COUNT(*) FROM {table_name}')
    count = cursor.fetchone()[0]
    return json.dumps({'table': table_name, 'row_count': count})
 
@tool
def get_null_rate(table_name: str, column_name: str) -> str:
    """Calculate the null rate for a specific column in an SAP HANA table."""
    conn = get_hana_connection()
    cursor = conn.cursor()
    cursor.execute(f'''
        SELECT
            COUNT(*) as total,
            SUM(CASE WHEN {column_name} IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) as nulls,
            ROUND(100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN {column_name} IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)
                / COUNT(*), 2) as null_rate_pct
        FROM {table_name}
    ''')
    row = cursor.fetchone()
    return json.dumps({
        'table': table_name, 'column': column_name,
        'total': row[0], 'nulls': row[1], 'null_rate_pct': float(row[2])
    })
 
@tool
def get_duplicate_rate(table_name: str, key_column: str) -> str:
    """Identify duplicate records based on a key column in an SAP HANA table."""
    conn = get_hana_connection()
    cursor = conn.cursor()
    cursor.execute(f'''
        SELECT COUNT(*) - COUNT(DISTINCT {key_column}) as duplicate_count,
               COUNT(*) as total_count
        FROM {table_name}
    ''')
    row = cursor.fetchone()
    return json.dumps({
        'table': table_name, 'key_column': key_column,
        'duplicate_count': row[0], 'total_count': row[1],
        'duplicate_rate_pct': round(100.0 * row[0] / row[1], 2) if row[1] > 0 else 0
    })
 
@tool
def generate_assessment_report(findings: str) -> str:
    """Generate a structured data quality assessment report from accumulated findings."""
    from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
    from langchain.schema import HumanMessage, SystemMessage
 
    llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='gpt-4o')
    response = llm([
        SystemMessage(content='''You are an SAP data quality expert.
        Generate a structured assessment report from the findings provided.
        Include: Executive Summary, Critical Issues, Recommendations.
        Be specific and actionable.'''),
        HumanMessage(content=f'Generate report from findings:\n{findings}')
    ])
    return response.content

Step 3 — Build the LangGraph Agent

# agent.py — LangGraph ReAct agent for SAP data quality assessment
from langgraph.prebuilt import create_react_agent
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
from tools import get_table_volume, get_null_rate, get_duplicate_rate, generate_assessment_report
 
# Initialize foundation model via SAP AI Core
llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='gpt-4o')
 
# Register SAP tools the agent can use
tools = [get_table_volume, get_null_rate, get_duplicate_rate, generate_assessment_report]
 
# System prompt — defines agent behavior and boundaries
system_prompt = '''You are an SAP data quality assessment agent.
Your goal is to autonomously assess the data quality of SAP HANA tables.

For each assessment:
1. Check the table volume first
2. Check null rates for key columns
3. Check duplicate rates on primary key columns
4. Flag any issue where null_rate > 5% or duplicate_rate > 0.1% as HIGH severity
5. Generate a structured findings report when assessment is complete

Always explain your reasoning before calling a tool.
Always summarize what you found after each tool call.
Stop when you have assessed all requested dimensions.'''
 
# Create the ReAct agent — LangGraph handles the reason/act loop
agent = create_react_agent(
    model=llm,
    tools=tools,
    state_modifier=system_prompt
)

Step 4 — Run the Agent

# Run the agent on an SAP table assessment task
from langchain_core.messages import HumanMessage
 
result = agent.invoke({
    'messages': [HumanMessage(content='''
        Assess the data quality of table MARA (SAP material master).
        Check volume, null rate on MTART and MATKL columns,
        and duplicate rate on MATNR. Generate a findings report.
    ''')]
})
 
# Print the agent's final response
print(result['messages'][-1].content)

The agent will reason through the task step by step — calling get_table_volume first, then get_null_rate for each column, then get_duplicate_rate, and finally generate_assessment_report with all accumulated findings. The reasoning trace is visible in the messages — each HumanMessage and AIMessage shows exactly what the agent thought and did.

What the Agent Output Looks Like

The agent produces a structured findings report with:

  • Executive Summary — overall data quality score and critical issues count
  • Findings per dimension — volume, nulls, duplicates with actual values
  • Severity classification — Critical, High, Medium, Low per finding
  • Specific recommendations — actionable fixes referencing actual column names and values

This output is the foundation for the migration assessment agent in Part 2, which extends this pattern across multiple SAP tables and produces a full landscape readiness report.

╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║         SAP DATA QUALITY ASSESSMENT AGENT — EXECUTION TRACE      ║
║         Table: MARA (Material Master — General Data)             ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝

[THOUGHT] I need to begin by checking the volume of the MARA table to
understand the scale of the assessment before evaluating data quality
dimensions.

[ACTION] get_table_volume("MARA")
[OBSERVATION] {"table": "MARA", "row_count": 847293}

[THOUGHT] The table contains 847,293 material master records. This is
a significant volume — quality issues even at low percentages will
affect thousands of records. I'll now check null rates starting with
MTART (Material Type), a critical classification field.

[ACTION] get_null_rate("MARA", "MTART")
[OBSERVATION] {"table": "MARA", "column": "MTART", "total": 847293,
               "nulls": 169, "null_rate_pct": 0.02}

[THOUGHT] MTART null rate is 0.02% — acceptable. 169 records without
a material type is low risk. Moving to MATKL (Material Group), which
drives procurement categorization and reporting.

[ACTION] get_null_rate("MARA", "MATKL")
[OBSERVATION] {"table": "MARA", "column": "MATKL", "total": 847293,
               "nulls": 73,969, "null_rate_pct": 8.73}

[THOUGHT] MATKL null rate is 8.73% — this exceeds the 5% threshold
and is flagged HIGH severity. 73,969 materials without a material group
will cause issues in procurement reporting, spend analytics, and any
AI classification pipeline that relies on MATKL for categorization.
This needs to be highlighted in the final report.

[ACTION] get_duplicate_rate("MARA", "MATNR")
[OBSERVATION] {"table": "MARA", "key_column": "MATNR",
               "duplicate_count": 0, "total_count": 847293,
               "duplicate_rate_pct": 0.0}

[THOUGHT] MATNR (Material Number) has zero duplicates — primary key
integrity is intact. I now have sufficient findings to generate the
structured assessment report.

[ACTION] generate_assessment_report(findings)

════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
SAP DATA QUALITY ASSESSMENT REPORT
Table: MARA — Material Master General Data
Assessment Date: 2026-03-28 | Agent: SAP Data Quality Agent v1.0
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

EXECUTIVE SUMMARY
─────────────────
Overall Data Quality Score: 74/100 — ATTENTION REQUIRED
Records Assessed: 847,293
Critical Issues: 0  |  High Severity: 1  |  Low Severity: 2

One high-severity data quality issue was identified that will impact
procurement reporting, spend analytics, and downstream AI pipelines
that rely on material group classification. Immediate remediation is
recommended before any S/4HANA migration or AI workload deployment.

FINDINGS
────────
┌──────────────┬──────────────┬───────────────┬──────────┬──────────┐
│ Dimension    │ Column       │ Value         │ Threshold│ Severity │
├──────────────┼──────────────┼───────────────┼──────────┼──────────┤
│ Volume       │ —            │ 847,293 rows  │ —        │ OK       │
│ Null Rate    │ MTART        │ 0.02% (169)   │ < 5%     │ LOW      │
│ Null Rate    │ MATKL        │ 8.73% (73,969)│ < 5%     │ HIGH ⚠   │
│ Duplicates   │ MATNR        │ 0.0%          │ < 0.1%   │ LOW      │
└──────────────┴──────────────┴───────────────┴──────────┴──────────┘

HIGH SEVERITY FINDING — MATKL (Material Group) Null Rate: 8.73%
73,969 of 847,293 material records are missing a Material Group
assignment. Impact areas:
  • Procurement spend reporting by category will be incomplete
  • AI-based material classification will produce unreliable results
  • S/4HANA migration data validation will flag these records

RECOMMENDATIONS
───────────────
1. [IMMEDIATE] Run MATKL gap analysis — extract all MATNR records
   where MATKL is null. Group by MTART to identify which material
   types are most affected. This scopes the remediation effort.

2. [SHORT-TERM] Assign Material Groups using the AI classification
   pipeline (Part 2 of Generative AI Hub series) — embed existing
   classified materials as reference examples and classify the
   73,969 unclassified records using RAG-augmented classification.

3. [BEFORE MIGRATION] Validate MATKL completeness reaches < 2%
   null rate before proceeding with any S/4HANA migration data
   load. Include this check in your migration go/no-go criteria.

4. [PROCESS] Add MATKL as a required field in material master
   creation workflows in MM01 to prevent future gaps.

════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
Agent completed assessment in 4 reasoning steps | 4 tool calls
Confidence Score: 0.91 | Human Review Required: NO
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
SERIES 2 ROADMAP 

What This Series Builds Across 6 Parts

 

Part

Title

What It Builds

Part 1

Concepts, Architecture, First ReAct Agent

Agent vs skill vs pipeline, LangGraph + AI Core stack, data quality ReAct agent on HANA

Part 2

SAP Data Migration Assessment Agent

Multi-tool agent that analyzes SAP landscape, scores migration readiness, produces findings report

Part 3

SAP Exception Log Triage Agent

Autonomous root cause analysis — agent queries logs, identifies patterns, recommends actions without human triage

Part 4

SAP Master Data Quality Agent

Detects data issues across master data domains, prioritizes fixes, generates remediation plan

Part 5

S/4HANA Readiness Agent

Assesses custom code and configuration against clean core principles, flags migration blockers

Part 6

Multi-Agent Orchestration

All four agents working together — an orchestrator agent delegates to specialized sub-agents and synthesizes a unified SAP landscape report

What's Next — Part 2

Part 2 builds the SAP Data Migration Assessment Agent — extending the ReAct pattern from a single table to a full landscape assessment across multiple systems, with conditional routing based on findings and a scored readiness report as output. The agent decides autonomously which systems to query, what to look for, and how to weight findings based on migration impact.

If you work through the ReAct agent in this article and want to extend it to your own SAP tables — drop a comment with your use case. The pattern generalizes across any HANA table and any assessment dimension. The tool definitions are the only thing that changes.

entry.title: Shape the Future of AI: Join the SAP Enterprise Service Management – Hands-on Experience
entry.content:
Are you ready to influence the next generation of SAP-managed integrations?

We are excited to announce two unique opportunities for customers and partners to get hands-on experience with
SAP Enterprise Service Management (ESM) and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. 
We are looking for participants to collaborate with us, validate new features, and provide early feedback that will directly impact
SAP’s product roadmap.
Whether you are focused on the technical "how-to" of onboarding or the functional power of Generative AI, we have a track for you. 

Choose Your Path
We are offering two SAP Integration Experience Feedback (IEF) tracks for our customers and partners.
You can participate in one or both, depending on their expertise:

Track 1: Provisioning & Setup (The Implementation Deep-Dive)

Focus on the end-to-end journey of getting the system up and running.

  • What you’ll do: Experience the SAP-managed integration and E2E customer onboarding, including SAP for Me
    and SAP Central Business Configuration (CBC).
  • The Goal: Help us automate and reduce manual effort in the Cloud ALM experience.
  • Best for: Project Leads, Business Process Consultants, and Technical Experts.
  • For Track 1 please register here.

Track 2: Functional & embedded AI Experience (The Business User Deep-Dive)

Focus on the business process and the power of embedded AI.

  • What you’ll do: Validate the "Backend Entity Extraction" feature - a Generative AI tool that automatically identifies
    dispute amounts, currencies, and reasons from emails.
  • The Goal: Evaluate AI Quality (Reliability, Relevance, and Robustness) and test the seamless data flow between
    SAP Enterprise Service Management and SAP S/4HANA     Cloud Public Edition.
  • Best for: Business Process Experts and those familiar with Dispute Management and Master Data Synchronization.
  • For Track 2 please register here.

Why Participate?

  • First-Hand Access: Get early access to the AI-embedded Dispute Management scenario
    as part of SAP Enterprise Service Management.
  • Influence SAP: Your feedback directly improves the quality and direction of SAP-managed integrations.
  • Recognition: Receive a confirmation of participation from SAP upon completion.

Engagement Details

  • Timeline: April – October 2026
  • Commitment: Includes an initial briefing, a kick-off demo, and 2 - 3 days of hands-on activities in a provided
    SAP landscape.
  • Location: Worldwide (Remote sessions)
  • Prerequisite: A valid Feedback Agreement (FBA) with SAP is required (if not available, we can help you set this up after registration!).


Looking foward to receive your registrations!
Your SAP Integration Experience Feedback Team

entry.title: 𝗔𝗜 and 𝗦𝗔𝗣, we should think more about it in another way
entry.content:
𝗔𝗜 and 𝗦𝗔𝗣: “How does AI fits to SAP?”
It’s becoming a real architectural conversation

-> What is AI ?
AI is not just automation. It’s about AI agents that can:
- Understand context
- Make decisions
- Take actions across systems
- Continuously learn and improve
=> This means digital co-workers inside your team and landscape

-> Where Does It Fit in SAP Architecture?
- Not replacing SAP
- AI sits as an intelligent layer on top of it

1. Core ERP -> The foundation
- SAP S/4HANA
- Finance, Supply Chain, Procurement, Sales
=> SAP continues to be the single source of truth

2. Business Technology Platform (BTP) -> The Enabler
- Integration Suite -> connects SAP & non-SAP systems
- Extension Suite -> build custom AI-driven apps
- AI Core & AI Foundation -> deploy models
=> SAP BTP will be the playground for AI agents

3. AI Layer -> The Brain
- LLM-powered agents (planning)
- Task-specific agents (Finance agent, Supply Chain agents, and more)
- Multi-agent orchestration
=> These agents don’t just analyze they are acting

4. Data Layer -> The Benzin
- SAP HANA / Datasphere
- Databricks, APIs, third-party systems
=> High-quality data = smarter decisions

5. Execution Layer -> Here the Value happens, agents trigger actions via:
- APIs
- SAP Fiori Apps
- Workflows
=> A Supply Chain agent detects delay → re-plans inventory → triggers purchase order →notifies stakeholders

6. Closed-loop intelligent automation (End-to-End Flow)
- Business event occurs in SAP 
- Agent picks up the signal via BTP
- AI evaluates options using historical (real-time data)
- Decision is made (maybe recommended)
- Action is executed in SAP
- Feedback loop improves future decisions

One example in theorie:
The Finance Agent = Auto-reconciles accounts, detects anomalies, suggests journal entries
The Procurement Agent = Chooses optimal vendor based on price, delivery, risk
The Supply Chain Agent = Predicts disruptions and autonomously re-plans supply
The Sales Agent = Recommends pricing strategies and next-best actions

7. Take care about possible challenges
- Data quality and governance model
- Security and compliance model
- Explainability of AI decisions
- Complexity and Integration
- Needed change management
=> AI is powerful and need an opion for controlling
=> AI will not replace anyone a human being
=> AI will unlock the next level of SAP ERP speed and intelligence
=> The future of SAP landscapes will not only be transactional, it will be:
- Self-optimizing
- Predictive
- Autonom

entry.title: SAP Agentic AI in Practice: Building the SAP Exception Log Triage Agent (Part 3 of 6)
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY  |  SERIES 2: SAP AGENTIC AI IN PRACTICE  |  PART 3 OF 6

SAP Agentic AI in Practice: Building the SAP Exception Log Triage Agent

From reactive triage to proactive monitoring — a multi-agent system with a router that delegates SM21 system log errors and SM37 job failures to specialized sub-agents, correlates findings across log types, and escalates autonomously.

By Deepa Dorairaj   |  SAP AI Solution Architect  |  Series 2, Part 3 of 6  |  Published 2026

TL;DR: Part 3 introduces the multi-agent pattern — a router agent that classifies incoming SAP errors and delegates to specialized sub-agents: one for SM21 system log analysis, one for SM37 job failure triage. The router also identifies correlations across log types that neither sub-agent sees alone. The article covers two stages: Stage A reactive triage (triggered on demand) and Stage B proactive monitoring (continuous, schedule-driven). This is the architectural foundation for the multi-agent orchestration in Part 6.

Series Context

This is Part 3 of SAP Agentic AI in Practice. Parts 1 and 2 built single agents — a data quality assessor and a migration assessment agent with conditional routing. Part 3 introduces multi-agent coordination: a router agent that classifies errors and delegates to specialized sub-agents. This pattern is the core building block for Part 6, where all agents in this series work together under a unified orchestrator.

The exception log triage use case is ideal for introducing multi-agent patterns because SAP error landscapes involve genuinely different log types that require different analysis approaches. A single agent handling SM21 and SM37 in the same reasoning chain produces lower quality findings than two specialized agents, each focused on their domain, coordinated by a router that sees the full picture.

WHY MULTI-AGENT FOR SAP LOG TRIAGE 

Why Log Triage Benefits from Multi-Agent Architecture

SAP exception triage in production environments involves multiple log sources that are related but structurally different:

Log Type

What It Contains

Who Cares

SM21 System Log

Platform-level errors — work process failures, memory issues, RFC connection problems, kernel errors. Context is technical infrastructure.

SAP Basis / Platform team

SM37 Job Log

Business process failures — scheduled job errors, batch processing failures, data load problems. Context is business process continuity.

Functional / Operations team

Correlation layer

When an SM37 job fails because of an SM21 RFC connection error — neither log alone tells the full story. The router sees both.

Both teams need joint view

A single agent reasoning over both log types simultaneously tends to conflate the contexts — applying infrastructure reasoning to business process errors and vice versa. Specialized sub-agents with a coordinating router produce more accurate findings and clearer escalation paths.

MULTI-AGENT ARCHITECTURE OVERVIEW 

Router + Sub-Agent Architecture

Error batch input → Router agent classifies → SM21 sub-agent → Correlate findings → Severity score + escalation
		→	SM37 sub-agent	→

The router agent has three responsibilities:

  • Classify incoming errors — determine which log type each error belongs to
  • Delegate to the appropriate specialized sub-agent
  • Correlate findings across both sub-agents — identify cases where SM21 infrastructure errors are causing SM37 job failures

 

Agent

Responsibility

Router agent

Classifies errors, delegates to sub-agents, correlates cross-log findings, produces unified severity score

SM21 sub-agent

Analyzes system log errors — identifies platform issues, RFC failures, memory problems, kernel errors

SM37 sub-agent

Analyzes job log failures — identifies which jobs failed, why, business process impact, retry feasibility

STATE SCHEMA AND TOOLS 

State Schema and Tool Definitions

Triage State Schema
# triage_agent.py — multi-agent exception log triage
from typing import TypedDict, List, Optional, Dict
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END
 
class TriageState(TypedDict):
    raw_errors:         List[dict]   # Incoming error batch
    sm21_errors:        List[dict]   # Classified SM21 errors
    sm37_errors:        List[dict]   # Classified SM37 errors
    sm21_findings:      List[dict]   # SM21 sub-agent findings
    sm37_findings:      List[dict]   # SM37 sub-agent findings
    correlations:       List[dict]   # Cross-log correlations
    overall_severity:   str          # CRITICAL/HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW
    escalation_needed:  bool
    escalation_targets: List[str]    # Which teams to notify
    triage_report:      Optional[str]

Tool Definitions

# tools/triage_tools.py
from langchain_core.tools import tool
import hdbcli.dbapi as hdb, json, os
 
def get_conn():
    return hdb.connect(address=os.environ['HANA_HOST'], port=443,
        user=os.environ['HANA_USER'],
        password=os.environ['HANA_PASSWORD'], encrypt=True)
 
@tool
def get_sm21_errors(hours_back: int = 24) -> str:
    """Fetch recent SM21 system log errors from SAP HANA."""
    conn = get_conn()
    cursor = conn.cursor()
    cursor.execute(f'''
        SELECT
            MANDT, DATUM, UZEIT, MTYPE,
            MSGID, MSGNO, MSGTX,
            REPID, ABAP_RUNTIME_ERROR
        FROM SYSLOG
        WHERE DATUM >= ADD_DAYS(CURRENT_DATE, -{hours_back // 24})
        AND MTYPE IN ('E', 'A', 'X')
        -- E=Error, A=Abend, X=Exit
        ORDER BY DATUM DESC, UZEIT DESC
        LIMIT 200
    ''')
    errors = []
    for row in cursor.fetchall():
        errors.append({
            'date': str(row[1]), 'time': str(row[2]),
            'type': row[3], 'message_id': row[4],
            'message_text': row[6], 'program': row[7],
            'runtime_error': row[8]
        })
    return json.dumps({'sm21_errors': errors, 'count': len(errors)})
 
@tool
def get_sm37_failures(hours_back: int = 24) -> str:
    """Fetch recent SM37 job failures from SAP HANA."""
    conn = get_conn()
    cursor = conn.cursor()
    cursor.execute(f'''
        SELECT
            JOBNAME, JOBCOUNT, STATUS,
            SDLSTRTDT, SDLSTRTTM,
            LASTCHGDT, LASTCHGTM,
            JOBCLASS, PRGNAME
        FROM TBTCO
        WHERE STATUS = 'A'
        -- A = Aborted
        AND SDLSTRTDT >= ADD_DAYS(CURRENT_DATE, -{hours_back // 24})
        ORDER BY LASTCHGDT DESC, LASTCHGTM DESC
        LIMIT 100
    ''')
    failures = []
    for row in cursor.fetchall():
        failures.append({
            'job_name': row[0], 'job_count': row[1],
            'status': row[2], 'start_date': str(row[3]),
            'start_time': str(row[4]), 'job_class': row[7],
            'program': row[8]
        })
    return json.dumps({'sm37_failures': failures, 'count': len(failures)})
 
@tool
def get_job_log_details(job_name: str, job_count: str) -> str:
    """Get detailed log messages for a specific failed SM37 job."""
    conn = get_conn()
    cursor = conn.cursor()
    cursor.execute(f'''
        SELECT MSGTY, MSGID, MSGNO, MSGV1, MSGV2
        FROM TBTCP
        WHERE JOBNAME = '{job_name}'
        AND JOBCOUNT = '{job_count}'
        AND MSGTY IN ('E', 'A')
        ORDER BY LNSNR
    ''')
    messages = [{'type': r[0], 'msg_id': r[1],
                 'msg_no': r[2], 'text': f'{r[3]} {r[4]}'.strip()}
                for r in cursor.fetchall()]
    return json.dumps({'job': job_name, 'messages': messages})
STAGE A: REACTIVE MULTI-AGENT TRIAGE 

Stage A — Reactive Triage on Demand

Stage A is triggered manually or by an integration call — it receives a batch of errors, classifies them, runs sub-agents, and produces a triage report. This is the right pattern for on-call support workflows and integration with ITSM tools like ServiceNow.

Router Agent

# Router: classifies errors and delegates to sub-agents
from langgraph.prebuilt import create_react_agent
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
 
llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='gpt-4o')
 
router_prompt = '''You are a SAP exception log router agent.
Your job is to:
1. Fetch recent SM21 system log errors using get_sm21_errors
2. Fetch recent SM37 job failures using get_sm37_failures
3. Count and categorize errors by type and severity
4. Identify the top 3 most critical issues requiring immediate attention
5. Note any patterns suggesting SM21 errors are causing SM37 failures
6. Produce a structured classification summary for sub-agent processing

Be systematic. Fetch both log types before drawing conclusions.
Always explain your classification reasoning.'''
 
router_agent = create_react_agent(
    model=llm,
    tools=[get_sm21_errors, get_sm37_failures],
    state_modifier=router_prompt
)

SM21 Sub-Agent

# SM21 specialist: deep analysis of system log errors
sm21_prompt = '''You are an SAP Basis expert specializing in SM21 system logs.
Analyze the SM21 errors provided and for each significant error:

1. Identify the root cause (infrastructure, memory, RFC, kernel)
2. Assess business impact — which processes are affected
3. Classify severity: CRITICAL / HIGH / MEDIUM / LOW
4. Recommend immediate action
5. Recommend preventive measure

Focus on patterns — a single error may be noise,
ten of the same error in one hour is a systemic issue.
Always reference specific error message IDs in your findings.'''
 
sm21_agent = create_react_agent(
    model=llm,
    tools=[get_sm21_errors],
    state_modifier=sm21_prompt
)

SM37 Sub-Agent

# SM37 specialist: deep analysis of job failures
sm37_prompt = '''You are an SAP operations expert specializing in SM37 job failures.
For each failed job:

1. Use get_job_log_details to retrieve the specific failure messages
2. Identify the root cause (data issue, RFC failure, timeout, authorization)
3. Assess business process impact — what does this job do?
4. Classify severity based on job class and business criticality
5. Determine if the job can be safely restarted or requires fix-first
6. Identify dependent jobs that may be blocked

Job class A = highest priority. Always investigate class A failures first.'''
 
sm37_agent = create_react_agent(
    model=llm,
    tools=[get_sm37_failures, get_job_log_details],
    state_modifier=sm37_prompt
)
Stage A Orchestration — Router + Sub-Agents
# Stage A: Orchestrate router + sub-agents reactively
from langchain_core.messages import HumanMessage
import json
 
def run_reactive_triage(hours_back: int = 24) -> dict:
    """Run full reactive triage across SM21 and SM37 logs."""
 
    # Step 1: Router classifies and summarizes all errors
    router_result = router_agent.invoke({
        'messages': [HumanMessage(content=f'''
            Fetch and classify all SAP errors from the last {hours_back} hours.
            Summarize SM21 system errors and SM37 job failures separately.
            Flag any cases where system errors appear to be causing job failures.
        ''')]
    })
    router_summary = router_result['messages'][-1].content
 
    # Step 2: SM21 sub-agent performs deep analysis
    sm21_result = sm21_agent.invoke({
        'messages': [HumanMessage(content=f'''
            Perform deep analysis of SM21 system log errors from
            the last {hours_back} hours. Router summary for context:
            {router_summary}
        ''')]
    })
    sm21_findings = sm21_result['messages'][-1].content
 
    # Step 3: SM37 sub-agent performs deep analysis
    sm37_result = sm37_agent.invoke({
        'messages': [HumanMessage(content=f'''
            Perform deep analysis of SM37 job failures from
            the last {hours_back} hours. Get job log details for
            class A failures first. Router summary for context:
            {router_summary}
        ''')]
    })
    sm37_findings = sm37_result['messages'][-1].content
 
    # Step 4: Router correlates findings from both sub-agents
    correlation_result = router_agent.invoke({
        'messages': [HumanMessage(content=f'''
            Correlate these findings from both log types and produce
            a unified triage report with:
            1. Overall severity (CRITICAL/HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW)
            2. Top 3 issues requiring immediate action
            3. Cross-log correlations (SM21 errors causing SM37 failures)
            4. Escalation targets (Basis team / Operations team / Both)
            5. Recommended actions in priority order

            SM21 findings: {sm21_findings}
            SM37 findings: {sm37_findings}
        ''')]
    })
 
    return {
        'router_summary': router_summary,
        'sm21_findings': sm21_findings,
        'sm37_findings': sm37_findings,
        'triage_report': correlation_result['messages'][-1].content
    }
 
# Run reactive triage
result = run_reactive_triage(hours_back=24)
print(result['triage_report'])

Passing the router summary as context to each sub-agent is the key pattern here. The sub-agents don't operate in isolation — they receive the router's classification as context, which focuses their analysis on the most significant errors rather than treating every log entry with equal weight.

STAGE B: PROACTIVE MONITORING AGENT 

Stage B — Proactive Monitoring with Scheduled Execution

Stage B wraps the Stage A reactive triage in a monitoring loop. The agent runs on a schedule, compares current error patterns against a baseline, and triggers triage automatically when anomalies are detected — without waiting for a human to initiate the process.

Scheduler triggers → Fetch current errors → Compare to baseline	→ Anomaly detected? → Run reactive triage	→ Escalate if needed

Baseline and Anomaly Detection

# monitoring.py — proactive monitoring wrapper
import json, time, os
from datetime import datetime
 
def get_error_baseline() -> dict:
    """Load stored baseline error counts for comparison."""
    # In production: store baseline in HANA or BTP configuration
    return {
        'sm21_hourly_avg': int(os.environ.get('SM21_BASELINE', '10')),
        'sm37_daily_avg': int(os.environ.get('SM37_BASELINE', '5')),
        'anomaly_threshold': 2.0
        # Trigger if errors > 2x baseline
    }
 
def check_for_anomalies(current_counts: dict, baseline: dict) -> dict:
    """Compare current error counts against baseline."""
    sm21_ratio = current_counts.get('sm21_count', 0) / max(baseline['sm21_hourly_avg'], 1)
    sm37_ratio = current_counts.get('sm37_count', 0) / max(baseline['sm37_daily_avg'], 1)
    threshold = baseline['anomaly_threshold']
 
    return {
        'anomaly_detected': sm21_ratio > threshold or sm37_ratio > threshold,
        'sm21_ratio': round(sm21_ratio, 2),
        'sm37_ratio': round(sm37_ratio, 2),
        'trigger_reason': (
            f'SM21 errors at {sm21_ratio:.1f}x baseline' if sm21_ratio > threshold
            else f'SM37 failures at {sm37_ratio:.1f}x baseline'
        ) if sm21_ratio > threshold or sm37_ratio > threshold else None
    }
 
def send_escalation(triage_report: str, targets: list):
    """Send escalation via Microsoft Graph API or Teams webhook."""
    # Integration with MS Graph covered in the training automation article
    # For BTP CF deployment: use requests to call Graph API
    print(f'[{datetime.now()}] Escalation sent to: {targets}')
    print(triage_report[:500])
    # Truncated for logging
 
def run_monitoring_cycle():
    """Single monitoring cycle — run on schedule via CF Scheduler."""
    print(f'[{datetime.now()}] Starting monitoring cycle...')
 
    # Quick error count check — lightweight, runs every cycle
    sm21_data = json.loads(get_sm21_errors.invoke({'hours_back': 1}))
    sm37_data = json.loads(get_sm37_failures.invoke({'hours_back': 24}))
 
    current_counts = {
        'sm21_count': sm21_data.get('count', 0),
        'sm37_count': sm37_data.get('count', 0)
    }
 
    baseline = get_error_baseline()
    anomaly = check_for_anomalies(current_counts, baseline)
 
    if anomaly['anomaly_detected']:
        print(f'Anomaly detected: {anomaly["trigger_reason"]}')
        # Run full reactive triage
        result = run_reactive_triage(hours_back=4)
        # Triage last 4 hours on anomaly
 
        # Escalate if triage confirms high severity
        if 'CRITICAL' in result['triage_report'] or 'HIGH' in result['triage_report']:
            send_escalation(
                result['triage_report'],
                targets=['basis-team@company.com', 'operations@company.com']
            )
    else:
        print(f'No anomaly. SM21: {current_counts["sm21_count"]} errors,
              SM37: {current_counts["sm37_count"]} failures — within baseline.')
 
# BTP CF Flask entry point — triggered by CF Scheduler
from flask import Flask, jsonify
app = Flask(__name__)
 
@app.route('/monitor', methods=['POST'])
def monitor():
    run_monitoring_cycle()
    return jsonify({'status': 'ok', 'timestamp': datetime.now().isoformat()})
 
@app.route('/triage', methods=['POST'])
def triage():
    # Manual reactive triage endpoint
    result = run_reactive_triage(hours_back=24)
    return jsonify(result)

The baseline values (SM21_BASELINE, SM37_BASELINE) are set as BTP environment variables in your manifest.yml. Start with conservative baselines — set them to your current average error counts — and tighten them over time as you observe the agent's sensitivity. Anomaly thresholds that are too low produce alert fatigue. Start at 2x baseline and adjust based on false positive rate.

STAGE A VS STAGE B 

When to Use Each Pattern

Consideration

Stage A — Reactive

Stage B — Proactive

Trigger

Manual or integration call

Scheduled — runs every N minutes

Use case

On-call support, ITSM integration, ad-hoc investigation

Production monitoring, overnight batch, continuous operations

Cost

Runs only when called — lower LLM cost

Runs on schedule — baseline check is lightweight, full triage only on anomaly

Deployment

REST endpoint on BTP CF

CF Scheduler + REST endpoint on BTP CF

Recommended for

Support teams, incident response workflows

Operations teams, 24x7 production environments

NEW PATTERN: ROUTER + SUB-AGENT COORDINATION 

What This Part Introduces for Part 6

Part 3 introduces the router + sub-agent coordination pattern that Part 6 uses to orchestrate all four series agents together. The key principles that carry forward:

  • The router has a global view — it sees all error types and identifies cross-domain correlations that sub-agents cannot see working independently
  • Sub-agents are specialists — they receive focused context from the router and produce higher quality findings than a generalist agent would
  • Context passing is explicit — each sub-agent receives the router's summary as input context, not just raw data
  • Correlation is the router's unique value — the pattern where SM21 infrastructure errors cause SM37 job failures is only visible to the agent with the cross-log view

In Part 6, this same router pattern becomes the orchestrator that delegates to the migration assessment agent from Part 2, the master data quality agent from Part 4, and the S/4HANA readiness agent from Part 5. The triage agent from this part also participates as a sub-agent when the orchestrator detects operational anomalies during assessment runs.

What's Next — Part 4

  • Part 4: SAP Master Data Quality Agent — detects and prioritizes data quality issues across master data domains (material, vendor, customer), generates a scored remediation plan, and integrates with the HANA vector store RAG pipeline from Series 1 for context-enriched findings.
  • Part 5: S/4HANA Readiness Agent — extends the Part 2 migration assessment with clean core analysis, deprecated function module and BAPI detection, and BAdI/enhancement spot mapping for upgrade impact scoring.
  • Part 6: Multi-Agent Orchestration — all four agents coordinated by a unified orchestrator that delegates, synthesizes, and produces a complete SAP landscape intelligence report.

The router + sub-agent pattern from this article is reusable across any SAP domain with multiple related data sources. The same architecture applies to financial audit log analysis, security violation detection across multiple authorization logs, and performance monitoring across work process and database logs.

entry.title: How I Connected Claude AI to My SAP ABAP System Using MCP — A Complete Windows Guide
entry.content:
Introduction

The SAP developer community has been buzzing about connecting Claude AI directly to live SAP ABAP systems using MCP (Model Context Protocol) — reading code, modifying objects, creating transports, and activating changes, all from a natural language chat interface.

I tried it myself on Windows, and it works. This blog walks you through exactly how to set it up.

What is MCP?

MCP (Model Context Protocol) is an open standard that lets AI models like Claude connect to external tools and data sources. Think of it as a plugin system for AI — instead of just answering questions from training data, Claude can actually call tools that interact with real systems.

For SAP developers, this means Claude can:

  • Read your actual ABAP code directly from your system
  • Write and modify objects
  • Run syntax checks
  • Create transport requests
  • Activate objects

All without you copy-pasting anything.

The Architecture

 
 
Claude Desktop / Claude Code CLI         ↕ MCP Protocol  ┌──────────────────────────┐  │   mcp-abap-adt           │  ← Read-only (13 tools)  │   mcp-abap-abap-adt-api  │  ← Full CRUD (30 tools)  └──────────────────────────┘         ↕ ADT REST API    Your SAP ABAP System

Both MCP servers were built by Mario Andreschak and communicate with SAP via the same ADT (ABAP Development Tools) API that Eclipse uses under the hood.

Prerequisites

Before starting, you need:

  • Windows 10 or 11 (64-bit)
  • An SAP ABAP system with /sap/bc/adt active in SICF
  • A user with SAP_ADT_DEVELOPER role (or equivalent)
  • A Claude Pro/Max/Team subscription at claude.ai
  • Internet connection

Step 1 — Install Node.js

Node.js is the runtime required by Claude Code CLI and the MCP servers.

  1. Go to https://nodejs.org and download the LTS version
  2. Install with all defaults
  3. Verify in Command Prompt:
 
 
node -v    → v24.x.xnpm -v     → 11.x.x

Step 2 — Install Git for Windows

Git provides the bash shell Claude Code requires on Windows.

  1. Go to https://git-scm.com/downloads/win and download the installer
  2. During setup, on the PATH screen select: "Git from the command line and also from 3rd-party software"
  3. Complete with all other defaults

Step 3 — Install Claude Code CLI

 
 
cmd
npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-codeclaude --version

Then log in:

 
 
cmd
claude

Select option 1 (Claude subscription), log in via browser, return to terminal.

Step 4 — Install mcp-abap-adt (Read-Only MCP Server)

This server gives Claude read access to your SAP system with 13 tools including GetProgram, GetClass, GetFunction, GetTable, SearchObject, and more.

 
 
cmd
mkdir C:\Toolscd C:\Toolsgit clone https://github.com/mario-andreschak/mcp-abap-adtcd mcp-abap-adtnpm installnpm run build

Create a .env file in the folder:

 
 
SAP_URL=https://your-sap-system.com:44300SAP_USERNAME=YOUR_USERNAMESAP_PASSWORD=YOUR_PASSWORDSAP_CLIENT=300TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0

Register with Claude Code:

 
 
cmd
claude mcp add mcp-abap-adt node C:/Tools/mcp-abap-adt/dist/index.jsclaude mcp list

You should see: mcp-abap-adt: ✓ Connected

Step 5 — Install mcp-abap-abap-adt-api (Full CRUD Server)

This is the powerful one — 30 tools including read, write, syntax check, activate, lock/unlock, and transport management.

 
 
cmd
cd C:\Toolsgit clone https://github.com/mario-andreschak/mcp-abap-abap-adt-api.gitcd mcp-abap-abap-adt-apinpm installnpm run build

Create .env (note: slightly different variable names):

 
 
SAP_URL=https://your-sap-system.com:44300SAP_USER=YOUR_USERNAMESAP_PASSWORD=YOUR_PASSWORDSAP_CLIENT=300SAP_LANGUAGE=ENNODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0

Register:

 
 
cmd
claude mcp add mcp-abap-adt-api node C:/Tools/mcp-abap-abap-adt-api/dist/index.js

Step 6 — Setup Claude Desktop (GUI Interface)

Claude Desktop is where it really comes alive — a beautiful chat UI like the LinkedIn video.

  1. Download from https://claude.ai/download
  2. Install and log in
  3. Go to ☰ → Settings → Developer → Edit Config
  4. Replace the content with:
 
 
json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "mcp-abap-adt": {
      "command": "C:\\Program Files\\nodejs\\node.exe",
      "args": ["C:\\Tools\\mcp-abap-adt\\dist\\index.js"],
      "env": {
        "SAP_URL": "https://your-sap-system.com:44300",
        "SAP_USERNAME": "YOUR_USERNAME",
        "SAP_PASSWORD": "YOUR_PASSWORD",
        "SAP_CLIENT": "300",
        "TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED": "0"
      }
    },
    "mcp-abap-adt-api": {
      "command": "C:\\Program Files\\nodejs\\node.exe",
      "args": ["C:\\Tools\\mcp-abap-abap-adt-api\\dist\\index.js"],
      "env": {
        "SAP_URL": "https://your-sap-system.com:44300",
        "SAP_USER": "YOUR_USERNAME",
        "SAP_PASSWORD": "YOUR_PASSWORD",
        "SAP_CLIENT": "300",
        "SAP_LANGUAGE": "EN",
        "NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED": "0"
      }
    }
  }
}

⚠️ Windows-specific tip: You MUST use the full path C:\\Program Files\\nodejs\\node.exe — using just "node" does not work in Claude Desktop on Windows. Also note double backslashes \\ in all paths.

  1. Kill Claude in Task Manager and reopen
  2. Go back to Settings → Developer — you should see both servers listed as running ✅

Step 7 — Add Free Official SAP MCP Servers

Bonus: Claude Desktop has a built-in directory of official SAP MCP servers. Click "Connect your tools to Claude" at the bottom of the chat, search for "SAP", and add:

  • SAPUI5 MCP Server — for UI5 development
  • SAP CAP MCP Server — for CAP projects
  • SAP Fiori MCP Server — for Fiori Elements apps

Also add these free hosted servers to Claude Code CLI (no installation needed):

 
 
cmd
claude mcp add --transport http abap-docs https://mcp-abap.marianzeis.de/mcpclaude mcp add --transport http sap-docs https://mcp-sap-docs.marianzeis.de/mcp

These give Claude access to the full ABAP keyword documentation across 8 SAP releases, ABAP cheat sheets, CAP docs, UI5 docs, and SAP Community posts.

Testing It

Open Claude Desktop, create a New Chat, and type:

 
 
Get the source code of program SAPMV45A

Watch Claude automatically call your SAP system, retrieve the source code, and explain it — all in seconds.

For write operations (test on $TMP objects only!):

 
 
Find a simple Z program in $TMP, add a comment at the top saying "Modified by Claude", save and activate it

For complex workflows like the LinkedIn video:

 
 
Connect to my SAP system. Copy class ZCL_SOURCE from package ZPKG_A into ZPKG_B, rename it to ZCL_TARGET, change the carrier_id in the SELECT for /DMO/CONNECTION, create a transport and activate.

Security Notes

  • Never commit .env files to Git
  • Change your SAP password if shared in screenshots
  • Use dedicated service accounts with minimum required authorizations
  • Only test write operations in $TMP before moving to real packages
  • The TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0 flag disables SSL validation — use only in dev environments
  • Check your company's AI policy before sending code to external APIs

My Experience

What surprised me most is how Claude handles the entire workflow autonomously. It doesn't just write code — it searches for the object, reads the source, understands the structure, reads class includes, creates the transport, locks the object, writes the code, runs a syntax check, activates, and unlocks. All from one natural language request.

 

Important Hint
